doctor.intro: I am Bykava Hanna, an osteopath since 2018. I believe in people and make this world a little better every day.

osteopathy.title: Osteopathy
osteopathy.text: Osteopathy is a holistic approach to health based on understanding the interconnections of all body systems. Every person is unique, and I approach treatment individually, taking into account the characteristics of your body and lifestyle.

reviews.title: Osteopath Warsaw — patient reviews | OsteoJump
reviews.hero_platforms: 100+ on Instagram • 40+ on Booksy • 10+ on Google
reviews.award_subtitle: Laureate of Orły Medycyny 2025 in Warsaw
reviews.award_description: This award is given for high ratings in the medical field. Thank you to all clients for your trust and reviews!
reviews.cta_thanks_text: Your reviews help others and inspire me to grow.

review1.text: Anna is a wonderful specialist, a true professional and a very pleasant person. I contacted her twice during the year. The first time was for my persistent back pain at that time. After the second session it became easier, after the fourth I forgot what a sore back was. Recently there was a problem with my neck. I didn't buy any ointments or pills, I immediately turned to Anna. Everything is fine now! I recommend everyone who is not yet familiar with osteopathy to come to Anna for an appointment.

review2.text: Hello! We contacted you in 2022 regarding our daughter's stuttering, and after the session everything passed)) she was 1 year and 11 months old

review3.text: Anna has gentle hands!! The entire session was a pleasure! Anna put my pelvis and ribs back in place after pregnancy (the ribs on one side, where the baby's bottom was during pregnancy, stuck out 2 cm higher than the other side). I feel light again now! Anna also adjusted my child's skull sutures as there was strong pressure during childbirth and one part of the skull overlapped the other, corrected torticollis and removed excessive tone in one arm. Everything else needs to be reinforced with exercises and massage. I needed one session, and my child needed 2. The child feels calmer and sleeps better 💖. I will definitely come back when I'm in Poland! Thank you 🙏

review4.text: I came with neck problems and a feeling of suffocation. After two or three sessions, I felt significant relief. Very glad I found Anna) Thanks to her wonderful hands, I feel much better! Thank you so much❤️

review5.text:
After the first session, I felt the anxiety and tension leave my body — tension that had been with me for a good decade. The steel hoop that squeezed my ribs even with a small breath disappeared, and I finally breathe fully 🥹

The session itself was very gentle, no discomfort or pain, it felt like you were just stroked and lightly massaged, but don't be fooled 😁 a day later such muscle soreness appears that it becomes obvious which deep muscles/ligaments were involved that hadn't worked in years.

Anna is very attentive, polite, pleasant, modest girl, which is very important for me — with medical education.

I recommend everyone to visit Anna at least once, this is, without exaggeration, a different quality of life.

Thank you so much 🫶

review6.text: Incredibly sensitive approach and super professional treatment. I highly recommend Dr. Hanna Bykava. She helped relieve a long, painful condition and established the necessary treatment rules. This was a life-changing session, and it was only the first one!

short2.text: "Anna is a great professional, very kind person"
short2.author: — Maria

short3.text: "Thank you Anna for the session, everything was great!"
short3.author: — Olga

short6.text: "I recommend with all my heart! Improvement in well-being right away"
short6.author: — Weronika

short8.text: "Everything was wonderful, stiffness and pain disappeared"
short8.author: — Anastasia K.

booking.confirmation: Your appointment is confirmed. We look forward to seeing you!

about_page.title: About Me
about_page.subtitle: Information about me coming soon
about_page.content: This page is under development. Soon information about me and my work experience will be posted here.
about_page.intro: Osteopathy is in my heart, in my hands, and in my thinking.
about_page.education: By basic education, I am a midwife and a laboratory diagnostics doctor, which gives me a deep understanding of physiology, biochemical processes, and interconnections in the human body.
about_page.experience: I have been practicing osteopathy professionally since 2018.
about_page.location: I live and work in Warsaw, where I see adults and children.
about_page.approach_title: In my practice, I use a comprehensive osteopathic approach, including:
about_page.conclusion: Every patient is unique to me. In my work, I strive not only to alleviate symptoms but also to find the root cause of disorders, helping the body restore balance and activate its own resources for self-healing.
about_page.see_education: For more details about education and certificates, visit the Education page.
about_page.hero_tagline: Helping babies, mothers, and adults gently and with care
about_page.children_text: I welcome little ones from their first days of life. I gently help with consequences of difficult births, muscle tone issues, torticollis, colic, sleep problems, and restlessness. It's important to me that every baby feels safe — I work tenderly, respecting each child's unique needs.
about_page.children_experience: I have experience as a midwife in a maternity hospital. I know what a child goes through during birth and understand how it can affect their body.
about_page.mothers_text: During planning — I help prepare the body for future changes. During pregnancy — I gently and safely help manage headaches, sleep problems, discomfort, and help the baby get into the right position. After birth — recover faster and restore the body to its normal state.
about_page.mothers_experience: I am a mother myself, so I truly understand what you are going through.
about_page.adults_text: Back and neck pain, headaches, consequences of injuries, chronic tension — these are the most common reasons adults come to me. I work with the body holistically — looking for the real cause, not just relieving symptoms. I also take on complex cases.
about_page.adults_experience: Including cases where traditional medicine falls short: migraines, panic attacks, reflux, TMJ issues, painful periods, chronic fatigue, light sensitivity, unstable blood pressure, and more.
about_page.journey_text1: My path into medicine began with midwifery. Working in a maternity hospital, I saw every day how babies are born — and how differently it can happen. That is when I began to understand how important what happens to a baby's body in the first minutes and days of life is.
about_page.journey_text2: Later I received my degree as a laboratory diagnostics doctor from Belarusian State Medical University. But I wanted to work with people directly — to help with my hands, not just with test results.
about_page.journey_text3: Everything changed when I discovered osteopathy. I was impressed by how gentle techniques can produce such results. Since 2018, I have been practicing osteopathy, constantly learning and improving.
about_page.journey_text4: Now I live in Warsaw, raising my daughter and helping patients — from newborns to adults.
about_page.motivation_text1: Modern medicine saves lives — and I am sincerely grateful for everything it has taught me. Protocols, standards, specializations — all of this was created to help millions of people. And it works.
about_page.motivation_text2: But sometimes I wanted more. Not because the system is bad — it is simply designed for a different scale. When you have hundreds of patients, it is difficult to give each one as much attention as you would like. It is no one's fault — just reality.
about_page.motivation_text3: Working in a maternity hospital, I saw how differently births can go. I saw babies who struggled in the first days. I saw mothers who needed support. And I often felt: I want to help more than the framework I am in allows.
about_page.motivation_text4: When I became a mother myself, this feeling only intensified. I understood how important it is for someone to look at you — specifically at you, not at a diagnosis or protocol.
about_page.motivation_text5: Osteopathy gave me this opportunity. Here I can take my time. I can see the whole person — their history, their body, their needs. I can work without templates, choosing what is right specifically for you.
about_page.motivation_conclusion: This is not a rejection of medicine — it is its continuation. Just in a different, more personal format.
about_page.approach_text1: Every session begins with a conversation. It is important for me to understand your history, complaints, and lifestyle. Then comes hands-on diagnosis: I assess tissue mobility, looking for tensions and restrictions.
about_page.approach_text2: I work gently. I use cranial, visceral, and structural techniques — depending on what you specifically need. My goal is not to force the body, but to help it restore balance.
about_page.approach_text3: After the session, I explain what I found and what you can do at home to maintain the results.
about_page.principle1_text: — delicate techniques suitable even for newborns
about_page.principle2_text: — no template protocols, only your body and its needs
about_page.principle3_text: — I view the body as a unified system
about_page.principle4_text: — I do not promise what I cannot guarantee
about_page.cta_text: If you are not sure whether osteopathy is right for you, or want to learn more — write to me. I will be happy to answer.
about_page.cta_location: I see patients in Warsaw, Ursus district. You can book through Booksy, WhatsApp, or Telegram.
about_page.booksy_rating: on Booksy — all reviews are positive
about_page.approach_brief_text1: Every session begins with a conversation and hands-on diagnosis. I work gently, using cranial, visceral, and structural techniques — depending on what you specifically need.
about_page.approach_brief_text2: My goal is not to force the body, but to help it restore balance. After the session, I explain what I found and what you can do at home.

appointment_page.title: Osteopath session — how it works | OsteoJump
appointment_page.hero_subtitle: What to expect from an osteopathy session
appointment_page.step1_text: Every session begins with a conversation. It is important for me to understand your history, complaints, and lifestyle. This helps build a complete picture and find the root cause of the problem.
appointment_page.step2_text: Then I perform hands-on diagnostics: I assess tissue mobility, looking for tensions and restrictions. This helps me understand which techniques will be most effective.
appointment_page.step3_text: I work gently. I use cranial, visceral, and structural techniques — depending on what you specifically need. My goal is not to force the body, but to help it restore balance.
appointment_page.step4_text: After the session, I explain what I found and what you can do at home to maintain the results. If necessary, I recommend a plan for further visits.
appointment_page.process_text1: Every osteopathy session is unique because every person is unique. I do not use template protocols — I work with what your body shows me in the moment.
appointment_page.process_text2: Osteopathic techniques can be directed at different structures: muscles and fascia, internal organs, skull and sacrum. The choice of technique depends on your problem and individual characteristics.
appointment_page.process_text3: Session duration is usually 45-60 minutes for adults and 30-45 minutes for children.
appointment_page.principle1_text: Delicate techniques suitable even for newborns. Working with the body without pain or discomfort.
appointment_page.principle2_text: No template protocols, only your body and its needs. Every session is adapted to you.
appointment_page.principle3_text: I view the body as a unified system. I look for the root cause, not just relieve symptoms.
appointment_page.principle4_text: I do not promise what I cannot guarantee. I am honest about possibilities and limitations.
appointment_page.cta_text: If you have questions about how a session works, or want to book — contact me.

faq.title: Osteopathy — questions and answers | OsteoJump
faq.subtitle: Frequently asked questions coming soon
faq.content: This page is under development. Soon answers to frequently asked questions will be posted here.
faq.hero_subtitle: Answers to common questions about osteopathy
faq.q1_answer: The session begins with a conversation and medical history, followed by hands-on diagnostics and gentle osteopathic work. Sessions last 45-60 minutes for adults and 30-45 minutes for children.
faq.q2_answer: Osteopathy is a method of manual diagnostics and treatment based on understanding the interconnections of all body structures. An osteopath works with muscles, fascia, organs, skull bones, and spine, helping the body restore its natural balance.
faq.osteo_vs_manual_title: How does osteopathy differ from manual therapy?
faq.osteo_vs_manual_answer: Manual therapy focuses on a specific area of pain, such as joints or the spine. Osteopathy looks for the root cause and works holistically — with fascia, internal organs and the craniosacral system. Both methods complement each other well.
faq.osteo_vs_manual_link: Read the comparison of osteopathy and manual therapy →
faq.osteo_vs_massage_title: How does osteopathy differ from therapeutic massage?
faq.osteo_vs_massage_answer: Therapeutic massage works with muscles — relieving tension and improving circulation. Osteopathy looks for the cause of tension and works holistically — with fascia, internal organs and the craniosacral system. The methods complement each other well.
faq.osteo_vs_massage_link: Read the comparison of osteopathy and therapeutic massage →
faq.osteo_vs_physio_title: How does osteopathy differ from physiotherapy?
faq.osteo_vs_physio_answer: Physiotherapy is a broad field — exercise therapy, electrotherapy, ultrasound, manual techniques. Osteopathy always works exclusively with the hands and always takes a holistic approach — we look for the cause, not just treat the symptom. The methods complement each other well.
faq.osteo_vs_physio_link: Read the comparison of osteopathy and physiotherapy →
faq.osteo_vs_chiro_title: How does an osteopath differ from a chiropractor?
faq.osteo_vs_chiro_answer: A chiropractor uses rapid spinal manipulations with a characteristic cracking sound. An osteopath works with gentle techniques — no sudden movements — and views the body as a whole, not just the spine. Osteopathic techniques are safe for children, pregnant women, and elderly patients.
faq.osteo_vs_chiro_link: Read the comparison of an osteopath and a chiropractor →
faq.osteo_tmj_title: Does osteopathy help with jaw pain and temporomandibular joint problems?
faq.osteo_tmj_answer: Yes. The temporomandibular joint is directly connected to the bones of the skull and the craniosacral system. An osteopath works with the root causes of the problem — tension in the skull, neck, and posture — not just the joint itself. I hold specialist training in craniosacral therapy (CST 1 and 2).
faq.osteo_tmj_link: Read about osteopathy for TMJ problems →
faq.q3_title: What does it feel like during a session?
faq.q3_answer: The sensations are gentle and pleasant — light pressure, relaxation, sometimes warmth. Work is done delicately, responding to your body. The techniques are so gentle they're suitable even for newborns and pregnant women.
faq.q4_answer: It depends on your problem and health condition. Often improvement is felt after the first session. For lasting results, 3-5 sessions with 1-2 week intervals are usually recommended.
faq.q5_answer: Comfortable clothes that do not restrict movement. A cotton t-shirt and light pants or leggings work well. Work is done with clothes on, no need to undress.
faq.q6_answer: Yes, osteopathy is safe for children of any age, including newborns. Children's techniques are even gentler and adapted to the characteristics of a growing body.
faq.q7_title: How do I book an appointment?
faq.q7_answer: You can book through Booksy, WhatsApp, or by calling. The 'Book Appointment' button is at the bottom of the page.
faq.q8_title: What's the difference between a consultation and a full session?
faq.q8_answer: A consultation (20 min) is an introduction. We'll discuss your concerns, I'll do an initial assessment, and explain how I can help. It's perfect if you're unsure or want to understand if osteopathy is right for you. A full session (55 min) is complete bodywork: detailed diagnostics and osteopathic treatment.
faq.contraindications_answer: There are few, but they exist: acute infections with fever, inflammatory processes, active cancer, fresh fractures and injuries, thrombosis, stroke or heart attack in acute phase, severe osteoporosis, serious cardiovascular conditions, early postoperative period. If you're unsure — write to me, we'll discuss your situation.
faq.anxiety_title: Are sessions safe for anxiety and panic attacks?
faq.anxiety_answer: Yes, moreover — osteopathy often helps with anxiety. Gentle bodywork releases tension, improves breathing, and helps the nervous system 'reset'. Many notice improvement after the first session.
faq.elderly_title: Is osteopathy suitable for elderly people?
faq.elderly_answer: Yes, osteopathy is suitable for people of any age. For elderly patients, I use especially gentle techniques, considering the body's characteristics. It helps with chronic pain, stiffness, and mobility issues.
faq.combine_title: Can osteopathy be combined with other treatments?
faq.combine_answer: Yes, osteopathy complements other methods well: physiotherapy, massage, orthodontic treatment, psychotherapy. It's important to tell me about all your procedures so I can coordinate the bodywork.
faq.insurance_answer: It depends on your insurance company and policy. Some insurance plans in Poland cover osteopath visits. I provide a receipt (faktura) that can be sent to insurance for reimbursement.
faq.reasons_title: What problems do people see an osteopath for?
faq.reasons_answer: Back pain, neck pain, headaches, migraines, injury consequences, chronic fatigue, digestive issues, sleep disorders, anxiety. Also — birth preparation, postpartum recovery, working with children from birth.
faq.stress_title: Does osteopathy help with stress and fatigue?
faq.stress_answer: Yes, chronic stress 'records' in the body: tense shoulders, restricted breathing, headaches. Osteopathy helps release physical tension, improving overall well-being and sleep quality.
faq.after_injury_title: Can I visit after injuries and surgeries?
faq.after_injury_answer: Yes, but after the acute recovery period. Osteopathy helps the body adapt to changes, works with scars and adhesions, restores mobility. Timing depends on the type of injury or surgery — we'll discuss individually.
faq.hernia_title: Does osteopathy work with hernias and protrusions?
faq.hernia_answer: Yes, for hernias and protrusions, osteopathy helps relieve muscle tension, improve blood supply, and reduce pain. I work gently, without sudden manipulations. The goal is to create conditions for recovery.
faq.orthodontics_title: Why see an osteopath during orthodontic treatment?
faq.orthodontics_answer: Bite is connected to posture, skull, and neck position. An osteopath helps the body adapt to changes from braces or aligners, relieves jaw tension, and improves orthodontic treatment results.
faq.pregnancy_planning_title: Is an osteopath needed when planning pregnancy?
faq.pregnancy_planning_answer: It can be helpful. An osteopath helps prepare the body for future changes: works with the pelvis, sacrum, improves blood supply to pelvic organs. Especially relevant for conception difficulties.
faq.pregnancy_issues_title: What can pregnant women see an osteopath for?
faq.pregnancy_issues_answer: Back and pelvic pain, heartburn, swelling, headaches, sleep disturbances, uterine tone. I also help the baby assume the correct position before birth. I work gently and safely at any stage.
faq.postpartum_when_title: When can I see an osteopath after giving birth?
faq.postpartum_when_answer: You can visit as early as 2-4 weeks after natural birth (after cesarean — 6-8 weeks). Early visits help the body recover faster and prevent chronic problems from forming.
faq.postpartum_reasons_title: What do women see an osteopath for after giving birth?
faq.postpartum_reasons_answer: Back and pelvic pain, diastasis, organ prolapse, urination issues, pain while breastfeeding, headaches, fatigue. Osteopathy helps the body return to form after pregnancy and childbirth.
faq.cesarean_title: Does osteopathy help after cesarean section?
faq.cesarean_answer: Yes, after the scar heals (usually 6-8 weeks). Osteopathy helps work with the scar and adhesions, restores tissue mobility, relieves tension in the abdomen and pelvis.
faq.pregnancy_frequency_title: How many sessions are needed during pregnancy and how often should I come?
faq.pregnancy_frequency_answer: There is no fixed schedule — it all depends on how you feel and your stage of pregnancy. In the first trimester, one visit for a general assessment is usually sufficient. In the second trimester, as the load on the body increases, I recommend coming every 3–4 weeks. In the third trimester, especially closer to delivery, every 2–3 weeks to prepare the pelvis and relieve tension. You can start at any stage of pregnancy if there are no acute medical contraindications. I tailor the number of sessions individually — some women need just 3–4 visits throughout the entire pregnancy, while others need more.
faq.pregnancy_safety_answer: No. Osteopathy during pregnancy involves very gentle techniques without sudden movements or pressure on the abdomen. I do not use forceful manipulations — I work delicately, listening to the body. My experience as a midwife helps me understand the physiology of pregnancy and sense the safe boundaries of treatment. There are situations when it is better to wait: threatened miscarriage, bleeding, or acute inflammation. In such cases, I will honestly tell you that now is not the time for a session and recommend seeing your attending doctor.
faq.birth_types_title: Does osteopathy influence the choice of birth type?
faq.birth_types_answer: An osteopath does not decide for you how or where to give birth — that is your decision together with your obstetrician-gynaecologist. My job is to prepare the body so it is ready for any scenario. Working with the pelvis, sacrum, and ligaments helps with natural birth but is equally beneficial if a caesarean is planned. My experience working as a midwife in a maternity hospital showed me that giving birth in a medical facility, where specialists and equipment are close at hand, is the safest option. If you are interested in upright birth or other alternatives, please discuss this with your doctor. I will help prepare your body, but the decision always remains yours.
faq.infants_reasons_title: What do parents bring infants to an osteopath for?
faq.infants_reasons_answer: Consequences of difficult births, torticollis, head asymmetry, colic, frequent spitting up, sleep disturbances, restlessness, breastfeeding latch problems. The earlier you visit, the easier it is to help the baby.
faq.colic_sleep_title: Does osteopathy help with colic and sleep issues?
faq.colic_sleep_answer: Yes, colic is often related to tension in the baby's tummy or nervous system. Gentle osteopathic work helps release this tension. Parents usually notice improved sleep and behavior after the first sessions.
faq.autism_title: Is osteopathy suitable for children with autism?
faq.autism_answer: Yes, osteopathy can help children with ASD. Gentle bodywork helps reduce sensory overload, improve sleep, and overall well-being. I work at the child's pace, considering their characteristics.
faq.children_reasons_title: What do parents bring older children for?
faq.children_reasons_answer: Posture problems, headaches, concentration issues, injury consequences, frequent colds, bedwetting, developmental delays. Osteopathy helps children grow healthy and develop harmoniously.
faq.after_session_title: What to expect after a session?
faq.after_session_answer: On the day of the session, I recommend resting and drinking more water. In the first 1-3 days, mild fatigue or temporary symptom increase is possible — this is a normal body response to changes. Improvement usually follows.
faq.worse_after_title: What if I feel worse after the session?
faq.worse_after_answer: Temporary worsening in the first 1-3 days is a normal reaction. The body is adapting to changes. If symptoms persist longer or cause concern — write to me, and we'll figure it out together.
faq.payment_methods_answer: I accept card, cash, BLIK, and bank transfer payments. Choose whichever is most convenient for you.
faq.blik_answer: Yes, BLIK payment is available. It's fast and convenient — just share the code from your banking app.
faq.discounts_answer: Yes! There are several options: Family packages — discounts when booking multiple family members together. Booksy — book through the Booksy platform, where occasional promotions are available. Individual conditions — for patients requiring regular long-term treatment (e.g., children with autism spectrum disorders — at least 2 times a month, from 6 months), we can discuss individual terms.
faq.gift_certificate_title: Can I buy a gift certificate?
faq.gift_certificate_answer: Yes! You can purchase a certificate online in just a couple of minutes. Choose a package (for an adult, child, pregnant woman, or family), fill in the details, pay securely via Stripe — and the certificate is ready to download. Valid for 3 months.
faq.booking_without_booksy_answer: Yes, you can book via WhatsApp, Telegram, or phone. Booksy is convenient for choosing your own time, but if you prefer personal contact — just write or call.
faq.no_convenient_time_title: I can't find a convenient time in the schedule
faq.no_convenient_time_answer: Write or call me — tell me what time works for you. I'll help find options, even if they're not currently shown in the online schedule.
faq.invoice_title: Can I get a receipt or invoice?
faq.invoice_answer: Yes, I provide an invoice (faktura) upon request. You can use it for insurance reimbursement or tax deduction.

hero.name: Hanna Bykava
hero.utp: I help when other methods don't work
hero.audience: Pregnant women, infants, children and adults

r1.title: Gentle yet deep impact
r1.text: Osteopathic techniques are so gentle that you can fall asleep during the session, yet they reach the deepest structures of the body

r2.title: Small changes — big effect
r2.text: Osteopathic techniques can achieve stunning results, especially compared to traditional medicine which is often powerless in certain cases

r3.title: Natural approach
r3.text: The human body has enormous capacity for self-healing. Osteopathy works to restore normal function of all systems and activate the body's internal healing processes

r4.title: Fresh perspective
r4.text: It often happens that you've seen all the doctors, done all the tests and examinations, yet you still feel unwell and rest doesn't help. You've come to the right place

r5.title: Small miracles
r5.text: Sometimes we can help conditions that seemed incurable or issues that appeared to require surgery

r6.title: Fast results
r6.text: Many feel improvement after just the first session

stats.review1: Anna is an excellent specialist, a true professional and a very pleasant person.
stats.review2: Anna has magic hands!! The whole session was a pleasure!
stats.review3: After just the first session, I felt my anxiety and body tension disappear.

education.title: Education
education.text: Medical university, specialization in osteopathy. Constantly improving my skills

experience.title: Experience
experience.text: 15 years in medicine, 7 years in osteopathy. Over 1000 patients

complex.title: Complex cases
complex.text: I take on unique cases where traditional medicine hasn't helped

approach.title: Individual approach
approach.text: No template protocols. I work with your body and its needs

results.title: Results and reviews
results.text: 5.0 rating on Booksy, 150+ reviews. Patients return and recommend to friends.

honesty.title: Honesty and transparency
honesty.text: I'll honestly tell you if I can't help. I don't make guarantees upfront and don't push unnecessary sessions.

motivation.title: Honest approach
motivation.text: Although many clients speak highly of my osteopathy sessions and recommend me to friends, I don't fully understand your situation yet, so I cannot make guarantees.

doubts.title: Your doubts are completely normal
doubts.text: If you have questions — ask them. I'll try to answer as honestly as possible. If I can help — great, if not, I'll tell you straight. I won't waste your time or push unnecessary sessions.

prices.alt_contact: If you don't use Booksy — contact me any way you prefer, we'll find a convenient time for your visit.

faq.text: You'll find more information about osteopathy and appointments on the FAQ page.

questions.title: Still have questions?
questions.text: Not sure what to expect? Have many questions? Check out the FAQ or book a consultation.

osteopathy_page.title: Osteopathy Warsaw — what it is | OsteoJump
osteopathy_page.hero_subtitle: A natural, gentle treatment method that addresses the root cause of problems, not just symptoms
osteopathy_page.principle1_text: The body is a unified system where everything is interconnected. A problem in one area can manifest as pain in another.
osteopathy_page.principle2_text: The body is capable of healing itself. The osteopath's task is to remove obstacles and help the body initiate this process.
osteopathy_page.principle3_text: A healthy body structure ensures healthy function. Restricted mobility leads to organ dysfunction.
osteopathy_page.principle4_text: An osteopath finds and eliminates the root cause of the problem, not just relieves pain. This provides long-term results.
osteopathy_page.step1_text: The osteopath assesses tissue mobility, finding areas of tension and movement restriction
osteopathy_page.step2_text: Identifies the root cause of dysfunction — often located far from the site of pain
osteopathy_page.step3_text: Using delicate techniques, restores mobility and balance in the body
osteopathy_page.cta_title: Discover how osteopathy can help you

consultation_page.title: Osteopathic consultation Warsaw — OsteoJump
consultation_page.hero_subtitle: The first step to understanding your situation
consultation_page.intro: If you've been searching for a solution to your problem but haven't found an answer yet, a consultation can be a good first step.
consultation_page.description: It's a calm, individual meeting with an osteopath where we carefully examine your situation, listen to you, and try to understand what's happening with your body and how we can help.
consultation_page.no_obligation: No treatment and no obligation to continue therapy.
consultation_page.includes_1: discuss your complaints and the history of the problem
consultation_page.includes_2: talk about what you've already tried and the results
consultation_page.includes_4: explain whether osteopathy can be helpful for you specifically
consultation_page.includes_5: describe how the treatment usually works and how many sessions may be needed
consultation_page.includes_6: discuss possible sensations and expected outcomes
consultation_page.result_title: As a result of the consultation
consultation_page.result_text: You'll gain a clear understanding of whether osteopathy is right for you and whether to start treatment.
consultation_page.result_target: Ideal for those who want to understand their situation and make an informed decision.
consultation_page.appointment_link: Learn how a full session works →

article_osteo_manual.title: Osteopathy vs Manual Therapy — Comparison | OsteoJump
article_osteo_manual.hero_title: How does osteopathy differ from manual therapy?
article_osteo_manual.hero_subtitle: Comparison of methods, differences in approach and when to choose the right specialist
article_osteo_manual.content:
What is manual therapy?

Manual therapy is a broad term covering various hands-on treatment techniques — joint mobilisations, spinal manipulations, soft tissue techniques. From the outside, a visit to a manual therapist and an osteopath may look similar: both work with their hands, searching for sources of pain and movement restrictions.

However, there are significant differences between these methods — both in diagnostic approach and in the scope of bodywork.

How does a manual therapist work?

Manual therapy usually focuses on the specific area of the problem — for example, the cervical spine or a painful joint. The therapist applies mobilisations, manipulations and muscle relaxation techniques to restore mobility and reduce pain in that area.

How does an osteopath work?

Osteopathy goes far beyond work with the musculoskeletal system. An osteopath:

  • Looks for the root cause of the problem, which is often far from the site of pain
  • Works with fascia — connective tissue that surrounds and connects all body structures
  • Uses visceral techniques — gentle work with internal organs that can affect posture and mobility
  • Applies craniosacral therapy — subtle work with the skull and sacrum, regulating tension throughout the body

The osteopathic approach is always holistic: rather than treating individual symptoms, the osteopath assesses the body as an integrated system and looks for patterns leading to complaints.

Gentle techniques — safe for everyone

Osteopathy does not use sudden movements or joint cracking. The techniques are gentle and delicate, making them suitable for:

  • Infants and small children
  • Pregnant women — at every stage
  • Elderly patients with sensitive tissues
  • Post-injury patients requiring a cautious approach

When to choose manual therapy?

Manual therapy works well when:

  • You have a clear diagnosis — e.g. a cervical blockage or limited mobility after an injury
  • The problem is localised — affecting a specific joint or muscle group
  • You need post-surgical rehabilitation — e.g. after knee arthroscopy

When to choose an osteopath?

Osteopathy can particularly help when:

  • The cause of pain is unclear — tests show no problems, but symptoms persist
  • Local treatment doesn't provide lasting relief — e.g. knee pain returns despite treating the joint alone, because the problem lies in the hip or pelvis; neck tension eases after mobilisation but returns within days, because the source is diaphragm tension
  • You have several complaints at once — headache, neck tension and digestive problems may have a common cause
  • Complaints are functional in nature — e.g. infant colic, sleep disorders, concentration problems
  • You're looking for care during pregnancy, postpartum or for a child — osteopathy offers safe, gentle techniques adapted to sensitive patients

Methods that complement each other

Manual therapy and osteopathy are not mutually exclusive — many people combine both approaches depending on their needs. Both manual rehabilitation and osteopathic therapy help in regaining health and wellbeing. The important thing is to find a specialist who accurately diagnoses the cause of the problem and selects the appropriate treatment.
article_osteo_manual.cta_title: Looking for manual therapy or osteopathy in Warsaw?
article_osteo_manual.cta_text: I invite you for a consultation — I'll assess how I can help and we'll agree on a plan together.
article_osteo_manual.cta_author: Hanna Bykava — osteopath, 15 years of medical experience. OsteoJump practice, Warsaw (Ursus).

article_osteo_massage.title: Osteopathy vs Therapeutic Massage — Comparison | OsteoJump
article_osteo_massage.hero_title: How does osteopathy differ from therapeutic massage?
article_osteo_massage.hero_subtitle: Comparison of methods, differences in approach and when to choose the right specialist
article_osteo_massage.content:
What is therapeutic massage?

Therapeutic massage is work with muscles and soft tissues. The massage therapist warms, relaxes and kneads the muscles to relieve tension, improve circulation and help you relax. Classic back massage, spinal massage, neck and shoulder massage — these are types of massage targeting a specific area of the body.

Massage deals well with consequences — muscle tension, spasms, fatigue. But it doesn't always reach the cause of why those muscles keep tensing up again and again.

How does an osteopath work?

An osteopath also works with their hands, but their task is different — to find why the tension arose, not just remove it. For example:

  • Back muscle tension may stem from problems in the fascia — connective tissue that links the entire body into one system
  • Lower back pain may be related to tension in internal organs — such as the intestines or kidneys
  • Tension headaches may come from disruptions in the craniosacral system — the skull and sacrum

An osteopath assesses the body as a whole and works with the cause, not just the symptom.

I know both approaches from the inside

I hold certificates in classic and therapeutic massage — I completed this training before studying osteopathy. That's why I understand well how massage works and where its capabilities end. During a consultation, I can assess what would be more suitable for you.

When to choose massage?

Massage works well when:

  • You want to relax and relieve tension after physical exertion or a stressful period
  • You're bothered by local stiffness — for example, a stiff neck after working at a computer
  • You want to improve circulation and overall wellbeing
  • You're looking for regular support — massage every 2-4 weeks helps maintain good condition

When to choose an osteopath?

Osteopathy can help when:

  • Massage helps but only briefly — back tension eases for a few days after a session, then returns. This signals that the cause lies deeper than the muscles
  • Back pain persists despite a series of massages — perhaps the source is not in the muscles but in fascia, internal organs or the pelvis
  • You have several complaints at once — back pain, headaches and digestive problems may have a common cause
  • Complaints are functional in nature — infant colic, sleep disorders, concentration problems in a child
  • You're looking for help during pregnancy, postpartum or for a child — osteopathy offers gentle, safe techniques

Methods that complement each other

Massage and osteopathy don't compete — they work well together. Osteopathy can find and eliminate the cause of the problem, while massage maintains the results between sessions. Many of my patients combine both approaches.
article_osteo_massage.cta_title: Looking for therapeutic massage or osteopathy in Warsaw?
article_osteo_massage.cta_text: I invite you for a consultation — I'll assess your situation and suggest what will be more effective.
article_osteo_massage.cta_author: Hanna Bykava — osteopath, certified massage therapist, 15 years of medical experience. OsteoJump practice, Warsaw (Ursus).

article_osteo_physio.title: Osteopathy vs Physiotherapy — Comparison | OsteoJump
article_osteo_physio.hero_title: How does osteopathy differ from physiotherapy?
article_osteo_physio.hero_subtitle: Two approaches to health — what sets them apart and when they complement each other
article_osteo_physio.content:
What is physiotherapy?

Physiotherapy is a broad medical field aimed at restoring mobility and bodily function. A physiotherapist draws on a wide range of tools: therapeutic exercises, electrotherapy, ultrasound, laser therapy, manual techniques, and kinesiotaping.

Typically, a physiotherapist works with a specific diagnosis and a referral from a doctor. A course of treatment may combine several methods and last weeks or months.

How does an osteopath work?

An osteopath works exclusively with the hands — no equipment, exercises, or additional tools. The task is to find where the mobility of tissues is restricted in the body, and restore it through gentle techniques.

The key difference lies in the diagnostic approach. An osteopath does not focus solely on the site of pain. If you have lower back pain, the cause may lie in restricted mobility of the internal organs, tension in the diaphragm, or even in the pelvic area. The osteopath looks for the root cause by assessing the body as a whole.

What is the essential difference?

Physiotherapy uses many methods and often focuses on a specific area — for example, strengthening the muscles around a damaged joint or reducing inflammation with ultrasound. This is particularly effective after surgery, fractures, and during rehabilitation.

Osteopathy always involves hands-on work and always takes a holistic approach. The osteopath does not treat a single joint or muscle — the aim is to restore balance throughout the entire body so that it can resolve the problem on its own.

When is it better to choose a physiotherapist?

  • After surgery or a fracture — when a rehabilitation programme with exercises is needed
  • When specific muscles need to be strengthened (for example, after a knee injury)
  • When equipment-based therapy has been prescribed (electrostimulation, ultrasound, laser)
  • During long-term rehabilitation that requires regular training

When is it better to choose an osteopath?

  • Pain keeps returning despite exercises and treatments — the root cause may not have been addressed
  • Chronic back pain, neck pain, headaches — when standard treatment does not produce lasting results
  • Problems affecting several areas of the body at the same time
  • Complaints related to pregnancy, childbirth, and postpartum recovery
  • Functional disorders in children — muscle tension, colic, torticollis, sleep difficulties

Methods that complement each other

Physiotherapy and osteopathy are not competitors. A physiotherapist can strengthen muscles and restore joint function. An osteopath can release the deep restriction that prevents the body from fully recovering.

Many patients combine both approaches: the osteopath works on the cause — the physiotherapist helps consolidate the result through exercise.
article_osteo_physio.cta_title: Not sure which method is right for you?
article_osteo_physio.cta_text: Book an appointment — I will assess your situation and advise on the approach that will be most effective.
article_osteo_physio.cta_author: Hanna Bykava — osteopath, 15 years of medical experience. OsteoJump practice, Warsaw (Ursus).

article_osteo_chiro.title: Osteopath vs Chiropractor — Comparison | OsteoJump
article_osteo_chiro.hero_title: How does an osteopath differ from a chiropractor?
article_osteo_chiro.hero_subtitle: Two hands-on methods — differences in techniques, approach, and safety
article_osteo_chiro.content:
What is chiropractic?

Chiropractic is a manual therapy method that focuses on the spine and joints. A chiropractor uses rapid, precise manipulations — short, forceful movements with a characteristic cracking sound — to restore the proper alignment of vertebrae and joints.

The central idea of chiropractic: when the vertebrae are correctly positioned, the nervous system functions properly and the body can heal itself. That is why spinal manipulation is the chiropractor's primary tool.

How does an osteopath work?

An osteopath also works with the hands, but the approach is broader. In addition to the spine and joints, an osteopath works with soft tissues, fascia, internal organs, and the craniosacral system (skull and sacrum).

Osteopathic techniques are gentle and fluid — no sudden movements, no cracking. An osteopath does not "set" a vertebra with a single thrust; instead, tissue mobility is gradually and gently restored, allowing the body itself to find the right alignment.

What is the essential difference?

Chiropractic focuses on the spine and joints, using rapid manipulations. This can be effective for acute blockages and restrictions.

Osteopathy views the body as a whole. If your back hurts, an osteopath will examine not only the spine, but also the internal organs, diaphragm, and pelvis — because the source of pain may be located far from the site of symptoms. The techniques are gentle, with no sudden movements.

Rapid spinal manipulations are not suitable for everyone. In certain conditions — osteoporosis, disc herniations, vascular problems in the neck — forceful movements require particular caution. Osteopathic techniques are gentle and adapt to the patient's condition, making them appropriate even in the most demanding cases.

When is chiropractic appropriate?

  • An acute spinal blockage — the feeling that something has "seized up"
  • You are already familiar with the method and it brings you relief
  • You need focused work with a specific joint or vertebra

When is it better to choose an osteopath?

  • You prefer a gentle approach without sudden manipulations — especially if the characteristic cracking sound concerns you
  • Pain keeps returning after spinal correction — if relief after an "adjustment" lasts only days or weeks and then everything repeats, this is a signal: the cause does not lie in the vertebra itself. An osteopath looks for why the vertebra "shifts out" — it may be due to tension in internal organs, fascia, or the pelvis
  • You need help during pregnancy or postpartum — gentle techniques are safe for both mother and baby
  • You are looking for a specialist for a child or infant — osteopathic techniques for children are exceptionally gentle
  • You have several complaints at once — back pain, headaches, digestive problems — an osteopath looks for the shared underlying cause

Safety and gentleness

Gentle osteopathic techniques are suitable for patients of all ages — from newborns to the elderly. No sudden movements, no cracking, no discomfort. This is particularly important for children, pregnant women, and those with heightened sensitivity.
article_osteo_chiro.cta_title: Looking for a gentle and safe approach?
article_osteo_chiro.cta_text: Book an appointment — I work with gentle techniques, suitable for adults, children, and pregnant women.
article_osteo_chiro.cta_author: Hanna Bykava — osteopath, 15 years of medical experience. OsteoJump practice, Warsaw (Ursus).

article_osteo_tmj.title: Osteopathy for Jaw Pain and TMJ Problems | OsteoJump
article_osteo_tmj.hero_title: Does osteopathy help with jaw pain and temporomandibular joint problems?
article_osteo_tmj.hero_subtitle: The temporomandibular joint, the craniosacral system, and a holistic approach
article_osteo_tmj.content:
What is the TMJ and why does it hurt?

The temporomandibular joint (TMJ) is the joint that connects the lower jaw to the skull. You use it every time you speak, chew, or yawn. When this joint does not function correctly, the following may occur:

  • Jaw pain, especially when chewing or opening the mouth
  • Clicking or grinding in the joint
  • Restricted mobility — the mouth does not open fully
  • Headaches, ear pain, or neck pain
  • Bruxism — involuntary clenching or grinding of the teeth
  • Visible changes in facial contours — asymmetry, jaw deviation

TMJ problems are often connected not only to the joint itself, but to tension in the surrounding structures — muscles, fascia, the cervical spine, and even the skull.

How does jaw physiotherapy work?

TMJ physiotherapy (jaw physiotherapy) focuses directly on the area around the joint. A physiotherapist works with the muscles of mastication, performs joint mobilisation, prescribes jaw exercises, and may use apparatus-based methods — ultrasound, electrostimulation.

This is an effective approach, particularly in acute conditions and following jaw injuries.

What can an osteopath do for TMJ problems?

An osteopath considers the TMJ not in isolation, but as part of a single system. The temporomandibular joint is directly connected to the bones of the skull, the cervical spine, and the craniosacral system — the system that links the skull to the sacrum through membranes and cerebrospinal fluid.

This is why, when working with the jaw, an osteopath may discover:

  • Tension in the temporal or facial bones, affecting the position of the joint
  • Restricted mobility in the cervical spine, which creates compensatory tension in the jaw
  • A disturbance in the craniosacral rhythm, connected to overall tension in the body
  • Postural problems that alter the position of the head and place stress on the TMJ

When should you see a physiotherapist?

  • Acute pain or jaw locking following an injury
  • You need specific exercises for the muscles of mastication
  • You have been recommended apparatus-based therapy for the TMJ area
  • You are undergoing orthodontic treatment and have been advised to have jaw physiotherapy

When should you see an osteopath?

  • Jaw physiotherapy helps, but the problem keeps returning — the cause may lie not in the joint itself, but in tension in the skull, neck, or the body as a whole
  • Jaw pain is accompanied by headaches, neck pain, or back pain — an osteopath looks for the shared underlying cause
  • Bruxism and teeth clenching connected to overall tension in the body
  • TMJ problems following orthodontic treatment — a change in the bite can affect the balance of the entire skull
  • Visible facial asymmetry or jaw deviation — an osteopath can work with the bones of the skull and the muscles influencing the position of the jaw and facial symmetry
  • You are looking for a gentle approach without painful intraoral manipulations

Craniosacral therapy and the TMJ

I hold specialist training in craniosacral therapy (CST 1 and 2), which is particularly effective in working with the TMJ. Craniosacral techniques act gently on the bones of the skull, the membranes, and the rhythm of the cerebrospinal fluid — this allows deep-seated tension to be released, which is often at the root of jaw problems.
article_osteo_tmj.cta_title: Are you troubled by jaw pain or clicking in the joint?
article_osteo_tmj.cta_text: Book an appointment — I will assess the condition of your TMJ in the context of the whole body. I work with gentle craniosacral techniques.
article_osteo_tmj.cta_author: Hanna Bykava — osteopath, certified craniosacral therapist (CST 1 and 2). OsteoJump practice, Warsaw (Ursus).

article_osteo_newborn.title: Osteopathy for Newborns and Babies | OsteoJump
article_osteo_newborn.hero_title: When does a newborn need an osteopath?
article_osteo_newborn.hero_subtitle: A gentle approach and working with babies from their very first days of life
article_osteo_newborn.content:
Why do parents bring their baby to an osteopath?

Birth is a serious physical challenge for a baby's body. The baby passes through the birth canal under significant pressure — the head adapts to the shape of the pelvis, the neck and shoulders are under considerable stress. Sometimes vacuum extraction, forceps, or a caesarean section are used. All of this can leave traces: tissue tension, restricted mobility, slight positional changes.

Most babies cope on their own. But sometimes symptoms appear that signal the body needs a little help:

  • Torticollis — the baby holds their head tilted or turned to one side
  • Muscle tone problems — hypertonia or hypotonia
  • Skull asymmetry — an uneven or flattened head shape
  • Colic and frequent spitting up
  • Restless sleep
  • Breastfeeding difficulties
  • Delayed motor development

An osteopath can gently release this tension — without medication and without causing pain.

Torticollis

Torticollis is one of the most common reasons parents seek osteopathic help. The baby holds their head tilted or turned to one side and is reluctant to turn the other way. Parents may notice that the baby always sleeps on the same side, or latches better on one breast than the other.

Causes can include the baby's position in the womb, the process of passing through the birth canal, or tension in the neck muscles and fascia. An osteopath works very gently — no sudden movements, no pain. Delicate techniques release tension in the neck muscles, membranes, and skull. The baby stays calm and often falls asleep during the session.

The earlier the better. In the first weeks of life, the tissues are at their most plastic and respond most readily to treatment.

Muscle tone problems

Parents may notice that the baby constantly clenches their fists, arches their back, or the whole body seems too tense — this is hypertonia. Or the opposite: the baby is floppy, struggles to hold their head up, and moves slowly — this is hypotonia.

These changes may be related to nervous system tension, the consequences of birth, or features of intrauterine development. An osteopath assesses the whole body — the skull, spine, and pelvis. Gentle techniques help normalise tone and improve mobility.

Head shape

After birth, a baby's head shape may be uneven — elongated, skewed, or asymmetric. This is normal: the skull bones are mobile and adapt during the birth process. In most cases, the shape evens out naturally in the first few weeks.

But sometimes this doesn't happen — especially if the baby consistently prefers lying on one side. Over time, plagiocephaly (a flat spot on the skull) may develop. An osteopath uses cranial techniques — very gentle work with the skull bones and membranes, using minimal pressure (literally just a few grams). This restores mobility in the sutures and creates conditions for the skull to align naturally.

Colic, poor sleep, frequent spitting up

The baby cries a lot, sleeps poorly, and spits up frequently — parents search for the cause. The paediatrician confirms the baby is healthy, but the symptoms persist.

The reasons can vary. Colic is often linked to an immature digestive tract or enzyme deficiency — for example, lactase deficiency. These conditions require paediatric monitoring.

But sometimes behind the colic, spitting up, and restlessness lies another cause — tension in the body from the birth process. Tension at the base of the skull can affect the vagus nerve, which regulates digestion. Tension in the diaphragm and chest can influence breathing and general wellbeing.

Osteopathic work gently releases this tension. Parents often notice that after treatment the baby becomes calmer, sleeps better, and feeds more easily.

What does a session look like?

A session lasts 30 to 45 minutes. The baby can be in their parent's arms, on a changing table, or on a soft treatment table. Feeding during the session is absolutely fine.

I work very gently — the pressure of my hands is like resting a hand on a closed eyelid. No pain, no sudden movements. Many babies relax and fall asleep during the session.

Before we begin, I ask about the pregnancy, the birth, the baby's behaviour, and whatever concerns you most. After the session, I explain what I found, what I worked on, and what to watch for at home.

When is the right time to come?

The earlier the better. The ideal window is the first 2 to 4 weeks of life, when the tissues are at their most plastic. But you can come at any age — whether your baby is a few months old or already one year. It may simply require a few more sessions.

A typical course is 2 to 4 sessions, spaced 1 to 2 weeks apart. The exact number depends on the baby's condition and is discussed after the first examination.

My experience with babies

Before training in osteopathy, I worked as a midwife in a maternity hospital. I was present at hundreds of births and understand first-hand what happens to a baby's body — the forces at work, the tensions that arise, and how a natural birth differs from a caesarean. I have specialist training in craniosacral therapy for children.

I'm also a mother — my daughter is eight years old. I remember the worries of those early months. I understand what parents feel when something doesn't seem right with their baby. This experience helps me work with the baby's body and support parents at the same time.
article_osteo_newborn.cta_text: Book an appointment — I'll gently assess your baby's condition and help. I work with newborns from their very first days of life.
article_osteo_newborn.cta_author: Hanna Bykava — osteopath, midwife, mother. Certified in craniosacral therapy for children. OsteoJump practice, Warsaw (Ursus).

article_osteo_pregnancy.title: Osteopathy During Pregnancy: What You Need to Know | OsteoJump
article_osteo_pregnancy.hero_title: Osteopathy During Pregnancy: What You Need to Know
article_osteo_pregnancy.hero_subtitle: Safety, pain relief and preparing your body for birth — from a specialist with midwifery experience
article_osteo_pregnancy.content:
Why pregnant women see an osteopath

Your body changes every day. New sensations, new questions, and sometimes new difficulties. Much of what arises during pregnancy is considered "normal", but that doesn't mean you simply have to endure it.

Here is what pregnant patients most commonly come with:

  • Back and pelvic pain
  • Leg swelling
  • Headaches
  • Heartburn and digestive problems
  • Insomnia and fatigue
  • Anxiety and tension
  • Breech or transverse presentation of the baby

An osteopath is not a replacement for your gynaecologist. It is a complement — gentle bodywork that helps you adapt to changes and feel better. But the first question almost everyone asks is: is it safe?

Is it safe?

Yes. During pregnancy, only gentle techniques are used — craniosacral therapy, fascial techniques, and soft tissue work. There are no sudden movements, no clicking, and no strong pressure.

To give you a sense of just how gentle the osteopath's touch is: the pressure of the hands is like resting a hand on a closed eyelid. That is enough for the tissues to respond, yet it is completely safe for both mother and baby.

There are situations where osteopathy is not recommended or requires particular caution:

  • Threatened miscarriage
  • Placenta praevia with bleeding
  • Severe pre-eclampsia

In all other cases, osteopathy is safe at any stage of pregnancy. Before the first session, I always ask in detail about your health and the course of your pregnancy. If anything gives me cause for concern, I will recommend consulting your doctor first.

Before osteopathy, I worked as a midwife in a maternity hospital. I know the physiology of pregnancy not only from textbooks — I saw it every day. This helps me understand precisely what is happening in the body at every stage, and to work with confidence and safety. You can read more about how an appointment works on a dedicated page.

When to come: the trimesters

First trimester (weeks 1–12). You can come, and it is safe. An osteopath can help with morning sickness, headaches, and tension in the body. Many women prefer to wait until 12 weeks — and that is perfectly fine too.

Second trimester (weeks 13–27). The ideal time to start seeing an osteopath. The body is changing actively: the bump is growing, the centre of gravity is shifting, and back and pelvic pain may appear. Osteopathy helps the body adapt to these changes.

Third trimester (weeks 28–40). Preparation for birth: work with the pelvis, sacrum, and diaphragm. Help with swelling and pain. If the baby is in breech presentation — this is the time to come. During this period, sessions every two to three weeks are appropriate.

For preventive purposes, one to two sessions per trimester is sufficient. With specific complaints, more frequent sessions may be needed as required. A session lasts 55 minutes.

Back and pelvic pain

This is the most common complaint during pregnancy. The growing uterus shifts the centre of gravity, the hormone relaxin softens the ligaments, and the load on the spine and pelvis increases with every week.

What women describe:

  • Lower back pain — a pulling, aching sensation that worsens towards the evening
  • Sacral pain — particularly after prolonged standing or walking
  • Symphysis pubis dysfunction — pain at the pubic joint, difficulty spreading the legs
  • Sciatica — a shooting pain from the lower back down the leg

An osteopath works with the pelvis, sacrum, lumbar spine, and diaphragm. The aim is to restore balance, release tension, and allow the body to distribute load more effectively.

Many women notice relief after the very first session. But it is important to understand: the body continues to change, and periodic support helps you navigate these changes with less discomfort.

Morning sickness, heartburn and digestion

Morning sickness. Nausea in the first trimester can be linked to heightened sensitivity of the vagus nerve. Craniosacral therapy helps reduce irritation of the nervous system and lessen the intensity of nausea.

Heartburn. The growing uterus pushes the stomach upward, and pressure on the diaphragm increases. An osteopath works with the diaphragm and thoracic region, helping to relieve compression and ease symptoms.

Constipation. Hormonal changes slow the bowel. Gentle visceral techniques help improve motility — without any pressure on the uterus.

Osteopathy does not replace diet or your doctor's recommendations, but it complements them — working directly with the body.

Swelling

Swelling during pregnancy is common, particularly in the third trimester. Blood volume increases, hormones cause fluid retention, and the growing uterus compresses the veins in the pelvis, impeding blood flow from the legs.

When to see your doctor urgently: sudden swelling of the face and hands, especially in combination with headache and raised blood pressure — this may be a sign of pre-eclampsia. Do not delay a visit to your gynaecologist.

If the swelling is the usual, moderate kind — an osteopath can help. Work with the diaphragm, pelvis, and thoracic region improves venous and lymphatic drainage. Many patients notice their legs feel lighter after a session.

Anxiety, insomnia and fatigue

Pregnancy is not only the joy of anticipation. It also brings anxiety: is the baby all right, how will the birth go, will I cope? Anxiety is not a weakness. It is a normal response to enormous change in life.

The problem is that stress and anxiety create tension in the body, and tension in the body amplifies stress. A vicious cycle results: anxiety — tightness in the neck, shoulders, and diaphragm — more anxiety — more tension.

Osteopathy helps break this cycle. Gentle bodywork releases physical tension, allowing the nervous system to shift out of "fight or flight" mode and into a state of recovery.

I have completed training in SomatoEmotional Release (SER) — a technique that helps address emotional tension held in the body. This is not psychotherapy; it is gentle somatic work that helps release deep-seated tension.

The results patients report: better sleep, less anxiety, more energy and inner resource.

Breech and transverse presentation

After 32 to 34 weeks, a baby usually settles into a head-down position — the optimal position for birth. But in roughly 3 to 4% of cases, the baby remains in breech (bottom down) or transverse presentation.

Why can an osteopath help? The baby's position depends not only on their "willingness to turn", but also on the space available in the uterus. If the mother's pelvis is asymmetric, the uterine ligaments are unevenly tensioned, or the diaphragm is restricted — it is physically difficult for the baby to turn.

An osteopath addresses these restrictions:

  • Restores pelvic symmetry
  • Releases tension in the uterine ligaments
  • Works with the diaphragm — creating more space for the baby

I do not turn the baby with my hands. I help the body create the conditions in which the baby can turn on their own.

The best time for this is between 32 and 36 weeks. Later on, there is less room to turn. And of course, there is no guarantee — sometimes there are objective reasons why a baby remains in breech presentation. But it is well worth trying.

Preparing for birth

Birth preparation in osteopathy means working with the body so that it is as ready as possible for the process of labour and delivery.

The pelvis. During labour, the baby passes through the pelvis. The more mobile the pelvic bones and sacrum, the easier this passage. An osteopath assesses and, where necessary, restores mobility in all the pelvic joints.

The pelvic floor. The pelvic floor muscles need to be supple — able to relax at the right moment. An osteopath works with the pelvic floor gently, through the surrounding tissues.

The diaphragm and breathing. Proper diaphragmatic breathing helps cope with contractions. If the diaphragm is restricted, breathing becomes shallow, and managing the pain becomes harder. An osteopath helps release the diaphragm.

It is best to start preparation from 32 to 34 weeks. Usually two to three sessions before the expected date of birth is sufficient.

As a former midwife, I know what happens during labour. I understand the biomechanics of the birth process and prepare the body for exactly what it is about to face.

My experience working with pregnant women

My path to working with pregnant women began long before osteopathy. I worked as a midwife in a maternity hospital — I witnessed hundreds of births and know the physiology of every trimester not from books, but from daily practice.

I have completed specialist training: craniosacral therapy for pregnancy and birth, and SomatoEmotional Release. This allows me to work not only with the physical body, but also with the emotional tension that so often accompanies pregnancy.

And I am a mother myself. My daughter is eight years old, and I remember everything clearly — the joy of expecting, the anxieties, the back pain, the sleepless nights. When a pregnant patient describes what she is feeling, I understand her not only as a specialist, but as a woman who has been through it herself.

Continuity of care is important to me: I work with a woman from pregnancy through birth preparation and beyond — with her newborn baby. This allows me to see the full picture and support the family at every stage.
article_osteo_pregnancy.cta_text: Book an appointment — I'll gently and carefully help your body adapt to changes and prepare for birth.
article_osteo_pregnancy.cta_author: Hanna Bykava — osteopath, former midwife, mother. Certified in craniosacral therapy for pregnancy and birth. OsteoJump practice, Warsaw (Ursus).

article_osteo_postpartum.title: Osteopathy After Birth: Postpartum Recovery | OsteoJump
article_osteo_postpartum.hero_subtitle: Diastasis, back pain, caesarean scar — gentle support for a body that has been through so much
article_osteo_postpartum.content:
What happens to the body after birth

For nine months your body changed to carry a baby. Then came the birth itself: an enormous effort for the whole organism. And now the baby is in your arms, while your body tries to return to how it was before. Sometimes this happens easily. Sometimes it does not.

Here is what women commonly experience after birth:

  • Back and pelvic pain — the body has not yet readjusted
  • Diastasis — separation of the abdominal muscles
  • Pelvic organ prolapse and urinary incontinence
  • Pain around the scar from a caesarean section
  • Headaches and neck pain — from feeding, carrying the baby, and sleep deprivation
  • Persistent fatigue and a feeling of exhaustion

The body knows how to recover — that is what it is designed to do. But after birth, new demands are placed upon it: feeding, carrying the baby, sleepless nights. The body does not have time to catch its breath, and small problems can become entrenched. Osteopathy gently guides the recovery process.

When you can see an osteopath

After a natural birth — from two to four weeks. The body has already begun to recover, and an osteopath can support that process.

After a caesarean section — from six to eight weeks. The wound needs time to heal. But it is worth not waiting too long — the earlier you start, the easier it is to work with the tissues.

If six months or a year has passed since the birth — it is not too late. The body remembers everything: the pregnancy and the birth. Restrictions that formed then may still be present now. An osteopath finds them and helps the body release the tension.

A session lasts 55 minutes — enough for thorough, unhurried work.

Diastasis of the rectus abdominis

Diastasis is the separation of the rectus abdominis muscles along the linea alba. During pregnancy, the growing uterus stretches the anterior abdominal wall, causing the muscles to move apart. After birth, they do not always return to their original position on their own.

Diastasis is not only an aesthetic concern. The separation of the muscles weakens support for the internal organs and spine, which can lead to lower back pain, postural problems, and a feeling of instability through the core.

Why does this affect more than just the muscles? Because the abdominal wall is connected to the pelvis, the diaphragm, and the pelvic floor. All of these work as a single system. When one part is weakened, the others compensate — and pain appears in the most unexpected places.

An osteopath does not work on the muscles themselves (that is the role of exercise), but on the conditions in which those muscles need to recover: pelvic balance, diaphragm mobility, and fascial tension. When the body is balanced, the muscles recover more effectively.

Osteopathy does not replace diastasis exercises — it complements them. Women often do all the right exercises but see slow progress, precisely because of imbalances in the body that an osteopath can address.

Caesarean section: the scar and adhesions

A caesarean section is a major operation. The incision passes through skin, subcutaneous tissue, fascia, muscles, the peritoneum, and the uterine wall. Each layer heals in its own way, and during the healing process adhesions may form — dense connections between tissues that would not normally be joined.

Adhesions can pull on surrounding structures: the bladder, the intestines, the uterine ligaments. This may manifest as:

  • A dragging pain in the lower abdomen
  • A feeling of tightness when bending forward
  • Pain during sexual intercourse
  • Digestive problems

An osteopath works with the scar and surrounding tissues gently — without pain or discomfort. The aim is to restore tissue mobility and reduce tension. Work can begin once the wound has fully healed (usually six to eight weeks after the operation), but even if years have passed, working with the scar is still possible and worthwhile. Tissues retain their capacity for change.

The pelvic floor and organ prolapse

The pelvic floor is a group of muscles that supports the bladder, uterus, and rectum. During pregnancy, these muscles carried an increasing load for nine months. Birth — particularly a prolonged or complicated one — can further weaken or damage the pelvic floor.

Symptoms that suggest a problem:

  • Urinary incontinence — when coughing, sneezing, or laughing
  • A feeling of heaviness in the lower abdomen, or a sense of something "coming down"
  • Discomfort or pain during sexual intercourse
  • Frequent urge to urinate

An osteopath does not work directly with the pelvic floor muscles. Instead, they restore pelvic balance: the sacrum, coccyx, iliac bones, and ligaments. When the framework is in place, the pelvic floor muscles find it easier to recover and perform their function.

Osteopathy complements Kegel exercises and work with a pelvic floor physiotherapist. It is not a replacement, but an important part of a comprehensive approach to recovery.

Back pain, neck pain and headaches

Once the baby arrives, a new set of demands is placed on the body. Feeding in awkward positions, carrying the baby, bending over the cot, the pram, sleep deprivation. The body has not yet recovered from the pregnancy and birth, yet it is already being asked to cope with entirely new challenges.

Common complaints:

  • Pain between the shoulder blades and in the thoracic spine — from feeding positions
  • Neck and shoulder pain — from tension and lack of sleep
  • Headaches — neck tension, postural changes, fatigue
  • Lower back pain — weakened abdominal muscles, a centre of gravity altered by pregnancy

An osteopath works with the thoracic spine, neck, and shoulder girdle — restoring mobility, relieving tension, and helping the body cope better with the demands being made of it. Many mothers put off seeing a doctor — "no time, I'll manage". But the longer the body compensates, the deeper these problems become entrenched.

My approach to postpartum recovery

Before osteopathy, I worked as a midwife in a maternity hospital. I witnessed hundreds of births — natural and caesarean, straightforward and complicated. I know what happens to the body during labour not from textbooks, but from daily practice.

This helps me understand what each individual woman's body has been through. A prolonged labour, a rapid labour, an episiotomy, an emergency caesarean — each situation leaves its own mark, and each calls for its own approach.

I believe in continuity of care. The ideal scenario is when I work with a woman during her pregnancy, then help her recover after birth, and subsequently check on the baby. This way I see the full picture and can be of the greatest possible help.

But even if we are meeting for the first time after the birth — that is perfectly fine. Every story is individual, and we begin from wherever you are now.

I am also a mother. My daughter is eight years old, and I remember everything well: the postpartum recovery, the sleepless nights, the back pain from endlessly carrying a baby in my arms. When a mother describes what she is feeling, I understand her not only as a specialist.
article_osteo_postpartum.cta_title: Have you given birth and feel your body needs help?
article_osteo_postpartum.cta_text: Book an appointment — I'll gently help your body recover after birth and adapt to your new life.
article_osteo_postpartum.cta_author: Hanna Bykava — osteopath, former midwife, mother. OsteoJump practice, Warsaw (Ursus).

article_osteo_preconception.title: Osteopathy for Preconception & Pregnancy Planning | OsteoJump
article_osteo_preconception.hero_subtitle: Preparing your body for conception and pregnancy — gently, carefully, ahead of time
article_osteo_preconception.content:
Why see an osteopath before you are pregnant

Most women come to an osteopath once they already have concerns: back pain during pregnancy, problems after birth. But there is a stage that is often overlooked — preparing the body before conception.

The logic is straightforward: pregnancy is a significant demand on the body. For nine months it will be changing, adapting, and carrying increasing weight. The better prepared it is, the smoother that journey will be.

Seeing an osteopath before pregnancy is not treatment. It is assessment and preparation: checking that everything in the body is working freely, and that there are no restrictions which might complicate the pregnancy or birth.

What an osteopath assesses and works with

The pelvis and sacrum. The pelvis is the foundation for a future pregnancy. The uterus sits within it, and the baby will pass through it during birth. If the pelvic bones are misaligned, the sacrum has limited mobility, or the ligaments are unevenly tensioned — this may create difficulties with conception, carrying the pregnancy, and birth itself.

The osteopath assesses pelvic symmetry and mobility, and where necessary gently restores balance.

The spine and posture. During pregnancy, the load on the spine increases with every month. If imbalances are already present — scoliosis, restricted mobility in the thoracic or lumbar spine — they will be amplified by pregnancy. Back pain that feels manageable now can become a serious problem during pregnancy.

It is better to address this in advance, while the body is not yet under that load.

Blood supply to the pelvic organs. The uterus, ovaries, and fallopian tubes all depend on a good blood supply. If the surrounding tissues are tense, the ligaments are tight, or the diaphragm is restricted — circulation may be compromised.

The osteopath works with the diaphragm, pelvic floor, and surrounding structures, improving blood flow and creating better conditions for conception.

The diaphragm and breathing. The diaphragm — the primary breathing muscle — plays an important role during pregnancy. As the uterus grows, it rises upward, and the diaphragm's mobility affects both breathing and the function of the internal organs. If the diaphragm is already restricted before pregnancy, things will be harder once pregnancy begins.

Particularly relevant when conception is proving difficult

If you have been trying to conceive for some time and medical investigations have not identified a problem — osteopathy may be a useful complement.

An osteopath does not treat infertility. But they can work with factors that do not show up on ultrasound or in blood tests:

  • Tension in the pelvis and uterine ligaments
  • Restricted mobility of the sacrum
  • Adhesions following surgery or previous inflammation
  • General tension in the body related to stress

Sometimes relieving tension and restoring balance is enough — and the body responds. This is not magic, and it is not a guarantee, but for many patients osteopathy has become part of their path to pregnancy.

Stress and the body

Planning a pregnancy is not only a time of hopeful anticipation. Especially if conception is not happening straight away. Anxiety and pressure — from those around you, or from within yourself — create tension in the body. And tension in the body can interfere with conception.

Osteopathy helps to break this cycle. Gentle work with the body releases physical tension, and the nervous system shifts into a restorative state. Many patients notice feeling calmer and more relaxed after a session.

I have trained in somato-emotional release — a technique that helps work with emotional tension held in the body.

What this looks like in practice

Usually one to three sessions is sufficient. At the first session comes the assessment: I evaluate the state of the pelvis, spine, diaphragm, and ligaments. From there we move to gentle work on any restrictions found. Each session lasts 55 minutes.

If pregnancy follows, we continue working together. I accompany women throughout pregnancy, help with postpartum recovery, and when needed work with the newborn.

For me, this is a complete continuum: preparation — pregnancy — birth — mother and baby. As a former midwife, I understand every stage of this journey.
article_osteo_preconception.cta_text: Book an appointment — I'll help prepare your body for conception and pregnancy with gentle osteopathic techniques.
article_osteo_preconception.cta_author: Hanna Bykava — osteopath, former midwife, mother. OsteoJump practice, Warsaw (Ursus).
